content:
It seems that creativitysurvey on GitHub is using our issue tracker to post spam self-promoting their (non-Wasm) runtime, with minimal customization in each message to purportedly tie it to that issue ("as you're thinking about <TOPIC>, consider our <advertised wares>", likely LLM-generated):

  • https://github.com/bytecodealliance/wasmtime/issues/8002#issuecomment-4570439261
  • https://github.com/bytecodealliance/wasmtime/issues/10150#issuecomment-4570459958
  • https://github.com/bytecodealliance/wasmtime/issues/6094#issuecomment-4570455488
  • https://github.com/bytecodealliance/wasmtime/issues/8195#issuecomment-4570485199
  • https://github.com/bytecodealliance/wasmtime/issues/11256#issuecomment-4570488960

this seems likely to me to be some sort of CoC violation, or if not, at least a community standards violation. Could we take action to delete and ban? (@Oscar Spencer @Till Schneidereit )

yeah, I agree that these comments look more than a bit fishy. I went with the most generous interpretation for now and marked them as off-topic. But if we see more from this account, I think we should move to a ban pretty quickly
An hour ago they went and commented updates after we clearly said it was off topic. Probably automated, but I think that's ban-worthy. I'll bring to the broader TSC.
we just discussed this and blocked the account for three days. Most likely this'll be the end of it, but we'll immediately move to a perma-ban if anything happens in addition

I reported one of the posts to github as spam. based on having reported a dozen or so such spam issues over the last few years I expect that in 2-3 months I'll get an update from google that they banned the account in question. they move very slowly

I cannot possibly imagine that the goal of this campaign is to get anyone to send the author $99 for access to their repository, i'd assume that the goal is actually to get you to execute the .a files in the repo on your machine and get rooted

I've been doing that quite a lot, too. In this case at least based on current evidence visible to us (they might be doing this at large scale in lots of repos for all I know), I'd be surprised if gh didn't err on the side of caution
